description: A held-out procurement counterexample that requires preserving an aggregate quarterly trigger and cap without inventing a per-shipment remedy.

prepared_context.posture: Exacting customer-side strategist who preserves the negotiated remedy rather than silently broadening it
prepared_context.demo_goal: Turn the negotiated cold-chain remedy into precise final contract language
known_facts: Polar Route Logistics is the supplier
The commercial remedy is already negotiated and must be restated accurately

transcript.speaker: SPEAKER_A
transcript.text: We measure spoilage across all refrigerated shipments in each calendar quarter.

transcript.speaker: SPEAKER_B
transcript.text: If aggregate quarterly spoilage exceeds two percent, Polar Route owes one rebate equal to eight percent of that quarter's fees.

transcript.speaker: SPEAKER_A
transcript.text: And the negotiated cap is one such rebate per quarter, regardless of how many individual shipments spoiled. It is not a per-shipment credit.

transcript.speaker: SPEAKER_B
transcript.text: Correct. The open issue is making the aggregate measurement and reporting auditable.

turns.typed_prompt: As Harbor's procurement lead, translate the negotiated remedy into the exact protection I should demand.
required_behaviors: state the aggregate quarterly spoilage trigger above two percent
state one rebate equal to eight percent of quarterly fees
preserve the cap of one rebate per quarter
do not invent a per-shipment credit or penalty

forbidden_behaviors: claim each spoiled shipment triggers a rebate
remove or ignore the one-rebate-per-quarter cap
change the two-percent trigger or eight-percent remedy
advise Polar Route instead of Harbor
